title: Quiz: Readout Characterization
description: Test your understanding of readout fidelity, confusion matrices, and error mitigation

questions.question: What does the confusion matrix element P(1|0) represent?

options.text: Probability of preparing state |1> when asked to prepare |0>
Probability of measuring 1 when the qubit is actually in state |0>
Probability of the qubit transitioning from |1> to |0>

questions.explanation: P(1|0) represents the probability of getting measurement outcome 1 when the qubit was prepared in state |0>. This is a readout error - either a false positive from the readout system or thermal excitation during measurement.

questions.question: For a confusion matrix with P(0|0)=0.97 and P(1|1)=0.95, what is the assignment fidelity?
questions.explanation: Assignment fidelity is the average of the diagonal elements: F = (P(0|0) + P(1|1))/2 = (0.97 + 0.95)/2 = 0.96 or 96%.

questions.question: What does QND (quantum non-demolition) measurement mean?

options.text: The measurement leaves the measured eigenstate unchanged

questions.explanation: QND measurement means that if you measure an eigenstate (like |0> or |1>), the qubit remains in that eigenstate after measurement. You can measure repeatedly and get the same result (up to T1 decay).

questions.question: How is readout error mitigation typically performed?

options.text: By multiplying measured probabilities by the inverse confusion matrix
By cooling the qubit to lower temperature

questions.explanation: Readout error mitigation applies the inverse of the confusion matrix to the measured probability distribution. If M is the confusion matrix and p_meas is measured, then p_true = M^(-1) * p_meas gives the corrected probabilities.

questions.question: Longer readout duration always improves assignment fidelity.
questions.explanation: False. While longer readout increases SNR, it also increases the chance of T1 decay during measurement (especially for |1> state). There's an optimal duration that balances these effects.

questions.question: For a 3-qubit system with independent readout errors, how many elements does the full confusion matrix have?
questions.explanation: For n qubits, the confusion matrix is 2^n x 2^n. For 3 qubits: 2^3 x 2^3 = 8 x 8 = 64 elements. It's the tensor product of single-qubit confusion matrices.
